title: Pappardelle al ragù di anatra
subtitle: Tuscan duck ragù over wide ribbon pasta
excerpt: Duck legs braised slowly with red wine, rosemary, and juniper until the meat falls from the bone, then shredded through a rich ragù and tossed with wide pappardelle. A Tuscan classic that improves overnight and freezes beautifully.
sourceNotes: Duck ragù with pappardelle is a canonical Tuscan preparation, particularly associated with the Arezzo and Siena provinces where duck is raised and hunted. Artusi includes a duck preparation in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain), and the wider tradition of braised game bird ragùs over egg pasta is documented throughout Tuscan regional cooking from the nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the ragù without pasta for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight and reheat gently.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the ragù and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The ragù improves markedly over 24 to 48 hours. Make it the day before and refrigerate.

content.text: Duck legs are among the best braising meats available. The fat under the skin renders into the cooking liquid; the meat, which is dark and muscular and well exercised, becomes falling-tender after 2 hours. The result, pulled from the bone and stirred through the reduced braising juices with a handful of Parmesan, makes a ragù that is richer and more complex than a beef version and significantly faster to make than a bolognese.
Pappardelle (the widest egg ribbon pasta) is the correct match. Narrower pasta lets the meaty flakes slide off; the broad ribbons hold them.

Pat the {{duck-legs}} dry with kitchen paper and season with salt. Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide casserole over medium-high heat. Place the duck legs skin-side down and press flat with a spatula. Cook without moving for 8 to 10 minutes until the skin is deep golden and most of the visible fat has rendered. Turn and brown the other side for 4 minutes. Remove and set aside. Pour off all but 2 tablespoons of the rendered fat.

Add the {{onion}} onion, {{carrot}} carrots, and {{celery}} celery to the casserole. Cook for 8 minutes over medium heat until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, the rosemary sprigs, the {{juniper-berries}} of juniper berries, and the {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Cook for 2 minutes.

Pour in the {{red-wine}} of red wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes, scraping up any browned bits from the base. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Return the duck legs, skin-side up. The liquid should come about halfway up the legs. Bring to a simmer, cover, reduce the heat to very low, and cook for 1 hour 45 minutes until the meat is completely tender and coming away from the bone.

Lift the duck legs onto a board. Remove and discard the skin, rosemary sprigs, bay leaves, and garlic cloves. Shred the meat from the bones into generous pieces. Return to the casserole. Taste and adjust seasoning. If the sauce looks thin, simmer uncovered for 10 minutes to reduce.

Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of pappardelle in generously salted boiling water until al dente. Scoop out 100 ml of pasta water. Drain. Toss with the duck ragù, adding a splash of pasta water if needed. Serve with the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan and an extra drizzle of olive oil.

items.symptom: Duck meat is tough after 2 hours
items.cause: Heat too high, causing the collagen to tighten rather than melt; or the legs were from a very old or large bird
items.fix: Continue at the lowest simmer; duck leg collagen needs sustained low heat — give it another 30 minutes

items.cause: Fat not poured off after browning; duck legs are fatty and release a great deal during rendering
items.fix: Pour off excess fat after browning; refrigerate the cooked ragù overnight and lift the solidified fat from the surface

items.cause: Too many berries, or berries not lightly crushed
items.fix: Use no more than 4 berries; crush them just enough to crack, not grind

items.symptom: Pasta breaks under the weight of the ragù
items.cause: Pappardelle overcooked, or the egg pasta was too thin
items.fix: Cook egg pappardelle to the lower end of the time range; it continues softening in the ragù

content.text: Duck ragù appears on the menus of every trattoria in Tuscany's hill towns from October to February. The Valdichiana valley, between Arezzo and Siena, is historically one of Italy's main duck-rearing areas, and the ragù reflects this geography: fatty, gamey, slow-cooked in local red wine. Pappardelle con la lepre (hare ragù) and pappardelle con il cinghiale (wild boar) occupy the same register. All three share the wide-ribbon pasta and the long braise; the game or bird changes with the season.
